Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 1
Oren Klaff: The reason we’re here together doing this now is for specifics. You can get the
generalities of Pitch Anything Strong: The Grand Architecture, from the book.
Then from the book, you can intuit how to do these things. But we’re here to get
very, very specific and tools to make this happen. So let’s get specific about The
Grand Architecture.
So, Step One is, you have to build a great landing page for your deal. In our
business, we call it a pitch deck, but whatever it is, it’s some physical or web
environment that people can learn about what you have—your deal, your product,
your idea, whatever it is you’re selling and pitching. So some of you may do that
on landing pages on websites. We do it with a PDF called a pitch deck.
And I’ll show you how we do it exactly in a minute, but the elements that we have
are these: Number One, an orientation to what the basic deal is. People, if you
sell lawnmowers, in the orientation you have to say it weighs 200 lbs. It has a
blade that spins at 20,000 rpm, and it can cut a quarter acre on a tank of gas. And
people will know it’s a home-based product. So guys who have a farm with 100
square acres know not to buy this lawnmower. You have to orient people to what
it is and who it’s for, so they don’t waste time with you. So One is the
orientation.
Number Two is the big idea. You have to give people the big idea. We’ve done
it in the S.T.R.O.N.G. series before, but I’ll review it for you. So a big idea can
be this—here’s a big idea. If you are able to build a semiconductor for cell
phones such that when you walk into a building, an apartment, or an office
complex, and you don’t lose signal—if you did that, then you would have billions
of people interested in your product, and Verizon, AT&T, and France Telecom
would want to buy what you have.
We in fact have that semiconductor, and we’ve built it into the iPhone and the
Blackberry, and France Telecom and AT&T and Verizon have done contracts
with us to buy it. And we are working on growing to a billion dollars in revenue
with our semiconductor company. Right? It’s a simple explanation of the big
idea. A semiconductor that doesn’t lose signal indoors. I’m selling it to big
wireless carriers. So you have to give the big idea.
In the landing page or the pitch deck, you also have to explain, why now? Why
not do this six months ago? Why wasn’t this done two years ago? Why shouldn’t
the investor or buyer just wait until it’s six months from now? Why do it now?
You have to explain that.
In the landing page or pitch deck, you have to give clear economics. You, the
buyer, pay this much. Over time, you make this much back. It costs you this
much along the way, and this is your return. You have to give clear economics.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 2
You have to give a clear value proposition. Who this is for. Why they should
buy it. How it is used. And how your life will be better after you buy it than it
was before. Whether it’s a jet fighter or a birthday cake, you have to give the
value proposition.
We give downside protection. Downside protection is, if you buy the deal, the
idea, or the product, how you’re protected from losing money, so at least you
won’t lose money. Or if you do lose money, it will just be a little bit, a little bit,
and not a lot.
And then we always try on the landing page or the pitch deck to give real insight
that shows this isn’t generic. We know what we’re doing. We’re inside this
market. We know this stuff better than anybody else. And if you work with us,
you can leverage the insight that we have.
Let me take you through a pitch deck now, and you can see exactly how we do
these things. I’ll now introduce you to velocity. This is the method I developed
as part of Intersection Capital, which is the firm I founded to help companies raise
capital and sell themselves. But really, it’s job is to bring as many prospects as
possible into Phase I of the Grand Architecture.
Again, if you recall, it’s important to have as many people or as many groups in
Phase I to work with, so when you get to the closing table, you have options.
Right? But many people ask, how do you get the most amount of people into
Phase I of the Grand Architecture process? And I’ll show you now what we’ve
developed.
So this is a company that we’re helping, and as you can see, we’ve packaged them
in a way that captures the big idea, in this case 187 words. And I’ll just go
through it. Think for a moment about what happens when you get a wound. You
expose the inside of your body to the outside world. What happens next? Germs,
bacteria, and other microbes flood in. And this is always bad news, because once
an infection happens, your chances of getting sick are getting worse minute by
minute. Your body will start to fight against the infection. Your doctor will pitch
in too—more about this later, but there’s one issue that could determine
80 percent of your success.
Is the infection within a base of biofilm? Unfortunately named, biofilm is not
actually good for you. It is the foundation for 80 percent of human wound
infections. If you could address the biofilm problem, you would have a major
opportunity to lower healthcare costs in the U.S. and even save lives. This pitch
deck is dedicated to the economic upside from [sic: of] solving the biofilm
problem.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 3
So unlike an executive summary or a business plan or a PowerPoint presentation,
what these velocity decks do—and I’m going to take you through one in full—is
capture the big idea. What’s the essence of the company?
Take you through another one now so you can see. This is a very interesting one.
We’re helping a company who has a completely altruistic purpose, and they go
into the most bio-diverse ecosystems in the world and try and help the countries
they’re in save them. Here’s the opportunity in 103 words.
To save humanity, you have to tackle some really nasty problems. You already
know about them. Climate change, clean energy, and species loss. To us, the
problem of species loss stands out. Why? Because it’s irreversible. Areas where
there is high species concentration have become exceedingly rare and valuable.
Out of 192 countries, only 17 make the watch list. None are in Europe. The U.S.
is one. The remaining 15 [sic: 16] are in South American, Central America,
Africa, and Asia.
All are at risk. Many are difficult to reach. Others are dangerous to operate in
once you reach them. Some are prohibitively expensive to deal with. Several are
targeted by other organizations, but there are four countries with areas of
bio-diversity, wealth, and species concentration that we have identified as
reachable, affordable, and savable. Our immediate focus is to defend these areas.
This prospectus describes one of our priorities. How we selected it and why it
must be defended and what it costs to do so. So again, this velocity conveys the
big idea. What’s going on here? What’s important? And what happens if you
succeed? These are incredibly important ways to help people understand that this
deal is worth looking at.
I’ll show you another quick introduction. The big idea in 143 words. If you want
to describe western North Dakota in just a few words, you might say fast-growing
economy or beautiful country or nice people. But if you really wanted to capture
what’s going on, you could simply say, “No vacancy.”
Now, this pitch deck and these big ideas are available in the Resources section,
and I recommend you look through them. Again, velocity is the way we bring the
most amount of people to Phase I of our process of the Grand Architecture.
I’d like to show you a little bit more of the Chelexa [sp?] deck, so you can
understand beyond the big idea how we show people about a deal and get them
excited about the deal. So we try to cover, first of all, that it doesn’t offend
people, and make them say, “What’s inside of this? I want to look inside.”
Here, you see a deal summary. It’s very important to tell people what they are
getting. Here, you may not be in the deal business, but we say who the CEO is,
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 4
what the product is, where they are, how much money they raised so far, and what
they need the money, and how big their market is. Right? We orient people to
the deal, so people looking at this can say “I’m interested” or “I’m not interested”
from these deal basics.
Next, we go into the big idea. I showed this one already. It’s very nice. What
happens when you get a wound? You expose the inside of your body to the
outside world. Following that, we show people that this is a disruptive
technology and it’s a big market, it’s a big market, and how much money is at
stake.
Moving forward, we show people again, quantify the size of the problem. Then
we try and show some provocative information and some insight of the market.
Here, we say, “It’s the fourth leading cause of death.” Most people don’t know
that, even sophisticated healthcare providers. And many people don’t know there
are only three options to treating a hospital-gained infection: remove it, kill it, or
cover it with dressings. So this is where we start to create some very simple
understanding of the product.
Next, many times if we give information-rich pages, then we try and provide
some eye candy so people are not reading facts and information constantly. As
we move through this deck, we give people and chance to understand what the
opportunity improve is on the current state of affairs. Moving forward, we show
people that if you are an investor in this market, how you could succeed with it.
Of course, we show the value proposition and exactly what the value of this
product is. We show how the company goes to market, and lastly we tell people
who the team is and who’s going to be executing. This is enough to get many
companies interested, many people interested, in looking at the deal, and it fills
the pipeline. Column 1, Phase I, the first phase of the Grand Architecture.
I’ll take you through one more quickly, and then you can explore these yourself in
the Resources section. So again, here’s Nature Culture. We showed you the big
idea. To save humanity, you have to tackle some really nasty problems, as we
said before. Here again, we start to see it repeated.
You can orient yourself to the project by the project summary. We give eye
candy, gives people a break from reading factual information. Then we tell
people what the problem is and do a little intrigue. “It’s not what you think.”
After the problem, we give the solution. Following that, we show exactly how the
business model works. A little bit more eye candy, then we like to give a clear
definition of exactly what it is.
We give some more detail because in this kind of business you have to tell people
exactly the locations you’re trying to save. More eye candy, and because this is a
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 5
business that pulls at the heartstrings, you can show even more eye candy. This is
one of my favorite pages, the jaguar. We show the management team, and that’s
it. You have now seen a full velocity deck. Again, this is in the Resources
section, and I encourage you to go look at that.
My final thoughts on this—whether you have a landing page, you have a pitch
deck, or a product brochure, it needs to be as compelling as possible. Get many,
many people into Phase I of the Grand Architecture.
As you can see, it’s super-important to build a great pitch deck or landing page to
bring as many people [sic: as possible] into your Phase I of your pitch process.
That’s Step One, you have to do that. Step Two is, you have to have a great,
strong pitch. S.T.R.O.N.G. Set the Frame, Tell the Story, Reveal the Intrigue,
Offer the Prize, Nail the Hook Point, Get the Deal. S.T.R.O.N.G. You have to
have a great pitch. This is the pitch that takes into account using time frames to
your benefit. And when you have other people trying to time-constrain you, you
know how to deal with it.
Prizing yourself, so even though you’re the seller and you’re offering the deal,
other people want to get in. They’re asking to get into your deal. I’m the prize,
you’re the commodity. You try and win my attention, even though I’m the seller.
So you setting time frames. You’re using prize frames, right? You recognize
analyst frames, when somebody’s trying to derail your presentation or your pitch
by being overly analytical. You know how to deal with that. Close distance on it,
shut it down.
You recognize the power frame when people are using their power and their
status and their authority and their celebrity and their influence and their money to
try and get concessions from you, to make you economically weak. You’ll
recognize that, that power frame, and you know how to close distance on it, deal
with it, shut it down.
Alright, the S.T.R.O.N.G. pitch is all these things. You know how to put your
deal in context of changing forces to give your big idea, to manage time frames,
manage attention, create tension when people aren’t quite paying enough attention
to you to put counter-pressure on them, to make them qualify themselves back to
you. Right? S.T.R.O.N.G. It does all these things. You know how to do this.
You go through it here in Pitch Mastery. But that’s Step Two to build your great
pitch. That’s what this is all about.
Step Three. You’ve got to use your pitch deck or your landing page and your
great S.T.R.O.N.G. pitch together to just attract as many potential deals or buyers
or JV partners or investors. You’ve got to attract as many as possible into
Phase I. You’re really, really going to do that by proliferating the landing page or
the pitch deck out in the market, as far and as wide as you can get. Bring in as
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 6
many people and then use your pitch to get them really into Phase I, where they
want what you have. You start working with them. Alright.
Step Four: You have to know what your transition actions are between Phase I,
Phase II, and Phase III, which is closing. I could tell you what ours are. They’re
probably very similar to yours, the way it is in your business. I’ll tell you what
ours are in my business.
To go from Phase I to Phase II, we need what’s called an expression of interest.
Sometimes an expression of interest is, “Oren, I love you and I’m going to do this
deal with you.” Sometimes it’s a term sheet. But it’s something that is solid, that
is either the expression of interest verbally or it is a physical document, but it
says, “We plan to advance. We don’t know if we’re going to close, and we don’t
know everything about you. We haven’t done due diligence yet, but based on
what we know, we like the pricing, roughly. We like the delivery times, roughly.
We like the value proposition, we like the economics. We like who you are, and
we want this.”
So moving from Phase I to Phase II is about wanting. For me, I can’t move
somebody from Phase I to Phase II unless they’ve expressed interest, either
verbally or physically in a document. And I’m never going to move somebody
into Phase II out of Phase I unless they said, “I want it.” So if somebody’s in
Phase I and they say, “Yeah, I’m kind of interested. I’ll give you a term sheet.”
That is not enough to move them into Phase II. I say, “Stop. You stay there.
You cannot advance until you say, ‘Oren, I love you and I am going to do this, if
it all comes true, the things you’ve said, and it works out in due diligence.’ ”
To move somebody from Phase I to Phase II, you need a—that’s a transition
point—you need something to happen. To move people from Phase II in to
Phase III, now you need something more solid. It’s not an expression of interest,
it’s, for us, it’s a purchase and sale agreement. For you, it might be a draft
purchase order, it might be a contract, it might be a very strong verbal agreement,
it might be a letter of interest. It’s not the closing document, but it’s something
that indicates very strong intent to move forward. And you also see wanting
escalate higher.
So now we’re in Phase III, which is really just prior to closing. To get someone
from Phase III into closing—for us, we need them to complete all of their due
diligence. No more questions. They’ve looked at every document. They’ve
looked at every technical detail they want to look at. They’ve asked every
question. No more questions. Alright, we’re through due diligence, and there’s a
setting of a closing date. No one is allowed to move out of Phase III. You see my
language? I allow people to move out of Phase III to buy something only when
they’ve met my requirements. They’ve completed due diligence. No more
questions, and there’s a setting of a closing date.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 7
Now you can come out of Phase III and go into closing. In a car sale, all this
might happen by the end of the day, right? If you sell those giant earth-moving
Caterpillar equipment, tractors, maybe it will be a month to do all this. For a
screenplay, I know some screenwriters, and maybe this takes six months or a year.
Phase I, Phase II, and Phase III might take a year for a screenwriter, but he still
has to do all these same things. Get as many people into Phase I with the landing
page. Choose what indicates a transition point. Get people transitioned into
Phase II. Get a stronger commitment. Transition people into Phase III. Pick a
closing date and make sure there are no more questions. And then get out of
Phase III into closing. So no matter what you have, you have to go through this
process.
In the online world, Phase I is an opt-in, right? Phase II is a live webinar, and
Phase III is the clicking of the “Order” button. So we all, whether we’re online or
we sell Caterpillar tractors or screenplays or are a large company, we all have to
go through this Grand Architecture.
The focus of this training is the S.T.R.O.N.G. pitch, but you have to know where
you are in the Grand Architecture in order to just pitch effectively. Number one,
for sure, you have to know where you are in the Grand Architecture to pitch
effectively because it’s very rare to have somebody who can do a one-and-done
pitch. They pitch one person perfectly, that person buys, and the whole process is
over. Hardly ever happens.
Typically, your pitch will get better and better and better over time with the more
humans you engage with it. So one is, you bring lots of people into the Grand
Architecture so you can pitch many times and get your pitch refined until it’s
actually working.
A second reason you need the Grand Architecture is what we started talking about
and the whole reason it actually came into existence. You have to eradicate your
own neediness. No matter how good you are—and I’m pretty da-- good, right,
maybe as good as it gets—and you have a big deal you’re trying to close, there’s
high stakes, and it’s likely that you’re going to act needy. If you do not have a
few options for people to close with, you will act needy.
And let me make this real for you. Imagine you’re selling a house. There’s only
one buyer that wants to move forward, and you go into the final negotiations on
price. The buyer says, “We’ve thought about it, and we want you to replace the
roof or we won’t go forward.”
“You want me to replace the roof? Why?”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 8
“Because we want you to do it or we’re not going to close.” If you’re like most
people, you concede, and you lose $15,000 or however much that costs.
However, if you have three very motivated buyers, and one of them says, “Hey,
we want you to replace the roof,” what do you do? You say, “Go kick rocks. I’m
not replacing the roof. You want a new roof? You buy it. These other two guys
aren’t getting a new roof for free. You’re not getting it either. So decide if you
want this house, because if you want it with me putting a new roof on it, you’re
not getting it.” And you say it that way, because you’re not needy.
So in one case, you have no other buyers and you say, “I’ll be glad to put a new
roof on it for you.” In the other case, you have a couple buyers and you say,
“You don’t get a roof. And I might just kick you out for being so rude to me.
You’re never going to get this house.” The difference between one and the other
is running good Grand Architecture.
So the third reason you need this, and I’m pretty convinced of this, is it’s
important to be in a process. When you’re doing S.T.R.O.N.G. or when you’re
doing tactics, when you’re focused on the nitty-gritty of pitching and selling and
explaining your idea, things are constantly changing around you. Your
circumstances are changing, and you’re adjusting to them. And you don’t know
where you are in the larger process.
The Grand Architecture gives you a mission. You have objectives you have to
reach, and you have goals. Now, tactics, S.T.R.O.N.G. and the pitching support
the mission and the objectives, but you have to know where you are in the big
picture.
Have you ever been deep in a project and gotten that fear in the pit of your
stomach that it’s not going to be good. You’re halfway through it and you’re like,
the book isn’t going to be good, or the design we’re working on, or the video
we’re working on, or the semiconductor we’re designing. I’m not sure it’s going
to be good, or I don’t know if it’s going to deliver on time, or maybe it will be
good but it will just be irrelevant. Nobody will want it anymore. And you get
this fear, like “I don’t know where I am. I know I’m lost. I know I’m doing good
work, but maybe this work is meaningless in the greater scheme of things.”
And then one of your partners, your boss, or your partner or someone you work
with, who’s holding the big picture in their mind, who’s not down in the weeds
with you, came in and then reoriented you to the process of why you’re doing this
thing and how it’s certain to be good and reconfirmed with you that you’re in the
right place at the right time doing the right thing. Right?
The Grand Architecture the way I’ve designed it is your tool for doing the same
thing. It’s when you get down in the weeds and you get lost, it’s your way of
knowing, “I’m doing the right thing and everything’s going to be okay. I’m in
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 9
good process.” The Grand Architecture lets you say to yourself or to you and
your partner, “I am in good process, and I don’t lose sight of the big picture,
which is get to the closing table with some options.”
It’s important to visualize the Grand Architecture. This is the process you’ll be
running, and you’ll of course be using S.T.R.O.N.G. when you pitch any
individual person or group. But you have to understand how to run good process,
because process is what an investment bank or investment advisor would do for
you, and his job is to help create as many closing options as possible for you so
you’re not needy at the closing table.
The Grand Architecture happens in three main phases, alright? Now what you’ll
do is you’ll start with Phase I, in which you initialize the deal. So you will take
your deal book or your pitch book, as in my business—this may be your landing
page in your business or even a product brochure—and you’ll show it to as big an
audience as possible, and you’ll do that to solicit their interest. And some people
will become very interested in your deal, and your deal will be initialized. And
you put those people in Phase I.
Now this is where you’ll use the S.T.R.O.N.G. method. Set the frame, tell the
story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize, and start to try and nail the hook point.
So you’ll work with these people and pitch them as well as can be done. Now,
someone in Phase I will say, “I’m ready to buy.” And you will say, “No, I’m not
going to go outside my process.” You won’t rush them to the closing table
because it’s likely at the closing table, if you have no options, they will retrade
you on price. They’ll ask for price concessions because they will sense that you
have no options. So this process is about creating options.
You’ll use S.T.R.O.N.G. to season these people and move as many of them as
possible up to, kind of, this closing line. So rather than letting anybody rush
quickly into Phase II, you’re going to work with them and get to a point of
advancement. A point of advancement is, you’re going to ask them to signal
some desire to move forward, and not everyone will move forward. Some will
fall off, and you have to be okay with that.
So you’re going to say, “Bob, Mary, Susan, group, I need you to specifically tell
me that you’re ready to move forward, you’re interested in the product, you’re
interested in the deal.” And some will come with you. So now you’ll have a
fewer amount [sic: number] of people to work with, but they will be somewhat
seasoned. At this point, you will continue to work with them, season them, try to
advance them in the deal.
But if anyone tries to say, “Hey, I want to rush off and close with you,” you’re
going to say, “No. That’s not my process. We’re just in Phase II of the process.”
So you’re going to move them up close to the edge here, and you’re going to look
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 10
for an expression of interest. In my business, that might be a letter of intent, a
memorandum of understanding, or even a contract. In your business, it might be
a purchase order. So you would say, “Hey, if you want to go further in this deal
at this point, you need to issue a draft purchase order or we need to put a draft
contract in place. And if you don’t do that by Friday—or by the 15th or in two
weeks or whatever it is—then you’re not going to be able to go forward in the
deal.”
And you can say that because you have options. And if any one of these people
says, “I’m ready to close,” you’re going to say, “No. We’re not into Phase III
yet.” And so now, you’re going to move some of them forward with the
expression of interest into Phase III. Not all of them will come with you. Now,
again, you’re going to continue to season these guys, and now they’ll be putting
pressure on you, right? They’ll be putting pressure on you to give concessions,
but you’re not going to go close any of them prematurely. You’re going to move
them to the edge, just as we did in the other phases, and now you’re going to look
for commitment to close.
Commitment to close. And this is what the Grand Architecture is all about,
getting to the point where you have options to move into closing. So if any one of
these individuals or groups says, “We want to change terms,” or “We want to
change price,” or “We want a price concession,” you have strength because you
have options. You say, “No, I’m not giving price concession, and if you don’t
want to move forward, I will close with someone else.” So now, you’re going to
apply that, and you will close with someone.
Now, if you sell products, you may close with multiple people. In the deal
business, typically you’ll only have one buyer. And this process will allow you to
get to the closing process with options and close with one person.
Let’s kind of review and take it from the beginning—so if we clear the screen.
The Grand Architecture, how to create as many closing options as possible. We
start with a large population, we move some of them into Phase I. Phase I is
when we initialize the deal, and we have kind of multiple interested parties. We
don’t take any party prematurely to the closing table. We bring them up to the
edge, and then we look for a point of advancement.
We get to the point of advancement, we make them signal their desire to move
forward, and they move into Phase II. In Phase II, we work to season the buyers
and work with them a little bit. And again, if any one buyer says he’s ready to
close, you say, “We’re still in process.” You move them up to the edge, and you
really say, “Now I need another point of advancement or an expression of
interest.”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 11
And this is something more significant than just a signal of desire. We’re really
looking for some kind of written expression of interest. It can be verbal in some
businesses, but in my business I really want someone to say, “Hey, here’s a letter
of intent, here’s a draft purchase order.”
Then you’re going to move some of those people—they’re not into Phase III,
they’re not all going to come with you. And we’re going to run the same process
over again. So now you’re going to have less [sic: fewer] people, but they’re
going to be very, very interested and seasoned in the deal. We use S.T.R.O.N.G.
to bring them up to the edge. They’re on the edge, and now we really have
options going into the closing where we’re going to ask for commitment to close.
If any one person tries to retrade us on price or terms, we’re going to say to them,
“We have other options. We’re going to close with someone else.” Then we’re
going to go into closing with one individual. That’s the Grand Architecture, and
it’s incredibly important to run this good process.
When I was raising $15 million for a project near Notre Dame University, I had a
time line of 35 days to raise all of the money, $15 million. Ten days into it, 10
days of the 35 days, I had raised $1 million, only $1 million. I started freaking
out. I was sure it could not be done, and I was becoming dejected. Because I
knew if I did not raise $15 million in 35 days, we would lose $1 million of our
own money, the deposit we put up.
And now I started going beyond the freak-out mode, right? And I was going
almost to the point of no return where I was scared, and scared is needy. Then my
partner John brought me a spreadsheet, and it was a breakdown of all of our last
10 deals, and every single one of them tracked to this exact same curve. A third
of the way in, we had only raised a little bit of the money. But we always got to
the end with all the money.
So this was my introduction to the Grand Architecture and its power to calm you
down, to orient yourself in space and time, and to allow you to work with poise
and strength and confidence. Inside Pitch Mastery is a Grand Architecture
worksheet. You and your business partners will use this to be able to focus on
giving great strong pitches, alright? The tactics. And keep yourself aware of
where you are in the grand process and how to get to the close without getting
needy or nervous or freaking out.
Most importantly, though, you will know not to be tempted, not to be tempted,
when one prospect in Phase I raises his arm and says, “I’m ready to close.”
Because you know that if you rush into closing with that guy, he will retrade you
at the closing table. So when he raises his arm prematurely and says he’s ready to
close, you say, “Sit down. You’re in my Grand Architecture. You play by my
rules. You stay in my process. And when we’re ready, we’ll let you know when
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 12 --

Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture	Grand Architecture
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 12
you can buy and what you have to pay.” That’s what the Grand Architecture does
for you. Because if anybody rushes to the closing, you know it will be bad for
you, so you bring everybody along so you have options. It lets you keep those
options.
It’s the Grand Architecture that allows you to do this.
